result:
The website is now built: **`pink-cybertruck-web-level-7.html`**. Level 6 is untouched (same 94,087 bytes, 18:49 timestamp).

My mistake earlier — I delivered the kit the skill asks for and stopped there. The kit's `scaffold.html` is deliberately a framework with empty `{{slots}}`; it's the thing you paste *into* a builder, not the finished site. The finished site is the new file.

## What I built

CYBR, rendered in teenage.engineering's design language rather than Tesla's. I drove it in a real browser and screenshotted every section, which caught three things I'd have otherwise shipped broken:

- The `film.png` poster wasn't a video frame at all — it was a screenshot of an entire earlier-level section, so the video tile was rendering a fake page nested inside the page. Removed it; the video now shows its own first frame (the pink truck on wet concrete).
- Anchor links jumped *under* the sticky nav, clipping each section heading. Fixed with `scroll-margin-top`.
- The trim badges were vertically cramped. Widened the padding.

## How the design language actually got applied

The whole page is a 980px poster that scales — there isn't a single raw pixel in any layout rule, only `calc(N * var(--px))`. Resize the window and the page grows as one object instead of reflowing. Every line-height is a multiple of the 10px baseline. Nothing is heavier than weight 300 except the display headline, corners are a hard `0`, and surfaces separate with 0.75px hairlines instead of shadows.

The biggest change from your earlier levels is what I *removed*. There's one animation on the entire page — the marquee ("stainless doesn't rust · pink doesn't apologize") — because that's the discipline that makes the source work. No gradients on the page background, no glow, no reveal-on-scroll. The truck sits on flat grey. The specs are a plain table with hairline rules, because teenage.engineering treats the spec sheet as a design element, and a truck site has every excuse to do the same.

The pink (`#FF3D8A`, your Flamingo) replaces the source's orange and behaves like ink: it appears on roughly three elements per screen — the word "Miami," the price, the trim badges — and nowhere else. The four-paint selector still works and repaints the truck vector live.

Both artifacts are yours to keep: the site itself, and `teenage-engineering-kit/` with the brief, tokens, scaffold, and source screenshot.
